names.japanese_name: カルボキシメチルセルロース

classification.sub_class: stabilizer, emulsifier, moisture retention agent, viscosity modifier

natural_occurrence.description: CMC is a semi-synthetic derivative of cellulose, created by chemically modifying plant cellulose with carboxymethyl groups. It's essentially processed and chemically modified wood pulp or cotton fiber.

manufacturing.description: Produced by treating cellulose with alkali (sodium hydroxide), then reacting it with monochloroacetic acid. This substitutes carboxymethyl groups (-OCH2COOH) onto the cellulose backbone. The sodium salt form (sodium carboxymethylcellulose) is highly water-soluble and forms viscous solutions. The degree of substitution determines viscosity and other properties.

other_uses.cosmetics: Thickener and stabilizer in toothpaste, lotions, shampoos, and cosmetics.
other_uses.industrial: Paper coating, textile sizing, oil drilling fluids, detergents, adhesives.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Tablet binder and disintegrant, ophthalmic lubricant, laxative.
other_uses.household: Toothpaste (most common use - creates smooth texture), laundry detergent, cleaning products.

eu.conditions: Widely permitted in most food categories; one of the most commonly used cellulose derivatives.

usa.notes: One of most widely used food additives

japan.japanese_regulation_name: カルボキシメチルセルロースナトリウム

commonly_found_in: Ice cream and frozen desserts (prevents ice crystal formation)
fun_facts: The same ingredient that makes your ice cream creamy is also in your toothpaste - CMC is in both food and oral care products!
Like all celluloses, CMC is basically chemically modified wood pulp or plant fiber - you're eating processed sawdust in your ice cream
CMC is one of the most versatile and widely used food additives - you've probably consumed it multiple times today without realizing it
It's completely indigestible and passes through your digestive system as dietary fiber with zero calories, making it perfect for low-calorie foods
The viscosity of CMC solutions can be incredibly high - a tiny amount (less than 1%) can create a thick, creamy texture
